segments.title: Why the Royal Navy plays Auld Lang Syne at passing-out parades
segments.summary: A brief explanation connects Auld Lang Syne with the Royal Navy passing-out parade at Britannia Royal Naval College and with comparable British service traditions.
segments.body: Clarke explains that Auld Lang Syne is not confined to New Year's celebrations: Royal Navy officers hear it at their passing-out parade as they march up the steps of Britannia Royal Naval College. He places the custom alongside its use for Royal Air Force officers at Cranwell and at the Sovereign's Parade at Sandhurst. The comparison makes the song a marker of transition from training into service across the British armed forces. He also interprets its themes as remembering old friends, acknowledging those lost, and looking forward to future friendships, which helps explain why it suits a graduation ritual.

evidence.note: Clarke describes the song's use at Royal Navy, RAF, and Army ceremonial parades and explains its themes of memory and friendship.
